message.content:
Adversaries may exploit legitimate external web services to host information that directs to additional command and control (C2) infrastructure, known as dead drop resolvers. These resolvers often contain obfuscated or encoded domains/IP addresses, allowing infected systems to connect and be redirected covertly. Commonly used platforms include Google, Twitter, GitHub, Pastebin, and Microsoft TechNet, which provide adversaries with cover due to their widespread use and SSL/TLS encryption. This technique enhances operational resiliency and protects C2 infrastructure from detection.

The impact of this technique includes persistent and stealthy C2 communication, making it difficult to detect and disrupt adversary operations. Adversaries such as APT41, Astaroth, BADNEWS, and others have been known to use this method, leveraging popular web services to hide in normal network traffic.

Mitigation strategies include monitoring and analyzing network traffic for unusual patterns, implementing strict web content filtering, and employing threat intelligence to identify and block known malicious domains and IP addresses. Regularly updating security tools and conducting thorough malware analysis can also help in identifying and mitigating the use of dead drop resolvers.
